title: The Manual Era of Knowledge Distillation: Turning Codebases into AI-Queryable Knowledge Vaults
summary: Using the Societas project as a case study, this chronicles the complete journey of building a repo advisor knowledge base from scratch—from analyzing the claude-code-advisor reference structure, to using parallel sub-agents to batch-generate dozens of architectural documents, to the pivotal moment of realizing this entire process could be automated.
body:
# The Manual Era of Knowledge Distillation: Turning Codebases into AI-Queryable Knowledge Vaults

Midnight strikes, and a command hits the terminal: "Take a look at /Users/touchskyer/Code/Jingxia/claude-code-advisor, the html and md files underneath—I want to do the same thing for societas." This single sentence kicked off a marathon session spanning three and a half days, 90 conversation chunks, and four new skills. But at the start, nobody knew it would go this far—the origin was just a simple need: make Claude understand the Societas project better.

## Why Knowledge Distillation Matters

Societas is a multi-module full-stack project—Python FastAPI backend, React + TypeScript frontend, Redis pub/sub for real-time communication, PostgreSQL storage. Every time I asked Claude about its architecture in a fresh session, Claude had to start from scratch: reading source code, guessing module boundaries, making tentative responses. The context window couldn't fit all the code from `backend/app/`, `frontend/src/`, and `tools/codeloop/` directories combined.

I'd already run a successful experiment with the claude-code project: distilling the codebase's key knowledge into structured markdown, then registering it as an advisor skill (`~/.claude/skills/societas-advisor/`). After that, whenever I asked architecture questions, Claude queried the knowledge base instead of re-reading source code. The results were good enough to replicate.

## The Reference: claude-code-advisor's Directory Structure

First step: enter plan mode and dissect claude-code-advisor's structure. Its core was a set of `.md` files under `docs/`, each covering a key facet:

```
docs/
├── 00-architecture-overview.md    # Global architecture, module dependency graph
├── 01-core-data-flow.md           # Complete chain: request → processing → response
├── 02-auth-and-session.md         # Authentication, session management
├── 03-tool-system.md              # Tool registration, invocation, permissions
├── ...
└── agent.md                       # Entry prompt, guides Claude on which docs to query
```

The key wasn't the number of documents, but the design of `agent.md`—it's a routing table telling Claude "if the user asks X, read document Y first." Without this routing, Claude would stuff all documents into context, making things slower instead.

## Document Inventory: Three Priority Tiers

After analyzing the reference, I drafted Societas's document inventory, organized into three priority tiers:

- **Critical** (3 docs): Project architecture overview, core data models (`backend/app/models/`), Agent-User interaction workflow
- **High** (6 docs): Module-level documentation—auth system (`backend/app/auth/`), frontend state management (`frontend/src/stores/`), real-time communication (Redis pub/sub channel design), tool system (`tools/codeloop/`), deployment config, API routes
- **Medium** (8 docs): Testing strategy, error handling, database migration records, environment variable documentation, and other cross-cutting concerns

Critical answers 80% of architecture questions. High solves "how does this module work." Medium handles the long tail.

## Parallel Sub-agents: Brute Force but Effective

Having one agent write 17 documents serially would be too slow. Here I leveraged Claude Code's sub-agent capability—dispatching multiple parallel tasks, each responsible for 1-2 documents.

Each sub-agent got a fixed prompt template:

```
Read the source code under {target_dir} and output structured markdown.
Requirements:
- Include specific file paths and function signatures (like backend/app/auth/jwt.py:create_token())
- Don't just restate code—answer "why this design" and "where to start if changing"
- Mark landmines (easy-to-miss gotchas)
```

This process wasn't smooth sailing. Output quality from sub-agents was inconsistent—some were too generic ("this module handles auth logic" kind of fluff), others copy-pasted code comments verbatim. The main agent had to review each output and reassign tasks that didn't meet quality standards.

The Medium priority 8 documents started on day two. By chunk 28 (roughly 36 hours in), the entire document set was basically complete.

## Quality Issues: Distillation ≠ Paraphrasing

The biggest trap in knowledge distillation is treating "read code then paraphrase" as distillation. Good advisor documents shouldn't answer "what does this code do," but rather:

1. **Why this design** — `backend/app/auth/` uses JWT + Redis blacklist instead of pure sessions because societas's multi-agent architecture needs stateless verification
2. **Where to start if changing** — Modifying the auth flow requires touching three files simultaneously: `jwt.py`, `middleware.py`, `frontend/src/stores/auth.ts`
3. **Where the landmines are** — Subprocess calls in `tools/codeloop/` lack timeouts; long-running tools will freeze the entire session

Inefficient stretches were obvious too: some chunks involved repeatedly sending "continue, fill in the High priority 6 docs too"—instructions not properly executed, forcing human repetition. In a three-day session, this friction adds up.

## The Leap from Manual to Automated

The real takeaway wasn't those 17 documents themselves. After manually completing the entire process, an undeniable pattern emerged: this process was too repetitive. Analyzing directory structure, drafting document inventory, dispatching sub-agents, reviewing quality, filling gaps—every step could be templated.

This insight directly spawned the session's second phase. Chunk 29's message said it all: "Wait wait, don't commit or push, help me turn this into a skill."

Thus `github-repo-advisor-builder` was born—a 6-phase state machine that automates the above manual workflow into a single command. But that's a story for another article.

## Limits of Knowledge Distillation

Finally, let me mention something imperfect. Static document distillation has a fundamental limitation: code changes, but documents don't auto-update. Today's advisor documents become stale when `backend/app/models/` adds new fields three months from now, or `frontend/src/stores/` switches state management approaches.

One approach is making it part of the CI pipeline—auto-redistill on every merge. But the cost isn't trivial—full distillation consumes lots of tokens each time, and quality isn't stable. Another approach is only distilling the "stable parts" (architectural decisions, core patterns) while letting the "volatile parts" (specific API signatures, config options) fall back to real-time code reading.

This problem wasn't solved when building repo-advisor-builder later either. It was consciously shelved—grab 80% of the value first, leave the remaining 20% of perfection for the future.
